policy.reason: External posting to LinkedIn/forums requires official API access, channel rules, rate limits, and human-quality checks. The autopilot prepares drafts but does not bot-post into third-party communities.

manualReviewQueue.title: Founder build note
manualReviewQueue.body:
I am building Workfusionapp for MT4/MT5 EA builders.

The current workflow:
- describe an EA idea,
- generate a complete MQL draft,
- paste MetaEditor compiler errors,
- get a corrected draft,
- risk-check before manual backtesting,
- download the MQL output.

It is not a signal service and does not promise trading performance.

Looking for serious MQL4/MQL5 builders who want to test it and send direct feedback.

https://www.workfusionapp.com

manualReviewQueue.title: Invalid stops reply
manualReviewQueue.body:
This is probably not the EA refusing the stop by itself. The trade server is rejecting the stop distance at the exact moment the EA sends or modifies the order.

For XAUUSD, a 10 point / 1 pip stop can easily become invalid if the spread is wider than the stop distance, or if the EA calculates the stop from the wrong side of the quote.

I would debug it this way:

1. Print Bid and Ask immediately before sending the order or modify request.
2. Print current spread in points.
3. Print SYMBOL_TRADE_STOPS_LEVEL and SYMBOL_TRADE_FREEZE_LEVEL.
4. For a buy position, confirm SL is below Bid by enough points.
5. For a sell position, confirm SL is above Ask by enough points.
6. Normalize SL/TP with the symbol digits before sending.
7. Print the exact retcode and request values for every failed trade request.

The important point: pending orders and manual tests can appear to work because the quote/spread changed. The EA must validate the stop against the live Bid/Ask and spread at the exact execution moment.

If useful, I wrote a focused checklist for this exact MT5 invalid-stops debugging path here:
https://www.workfusionapp.com/resources/fix-invalid-stops-mt5-ea?utm_source=mql5_forum&utm_medium=manual_reply&utm_campaign=invalid_stops_p0

manualReviewQueue.title: HistoryDeals compile-error reply
manualReviewQueue.body:
The first thing I would isolate is whether the root problem is the include or the history workflow.

In MT5, you usually do not need a random downloaded HistoryDeals.mqh file. The safer native path is:

1. Remove duplicate or manually downloaded HistoryDeals.mqh files.
2. Call HistorySelect(from, to) before reading closed deals.
3. Loop through HistoryDealsTotal().
4. Get each ticket with HistoryDealGetTicket(i).
5. Read fields with HistoryDealGetInteger, HistoryDealGetDouble, and HistoryDealGetString.
6. Group by DEAL_MAGIC and DEAL_SYMBOL if you are separating EA results.

The errors around MqlDeal, HistoryDealGetStruct, and ambiguous calls are often cascade errors after the wrong include or wrong API model.

If useful, I wrote a focused checklist for this exact MT5 history/deal debugging path here:
https://www.workfusionapp.com/resources/fix-mql5-history-deal-functions?utm_source=mql5_forum&utm_medium=manual_reply&utm_campaign=history_deals_p0

manualReviewQueue.title: CTrade undeclared identifier reply
manualReviewQueue.body:
Start with the first compiler error. If trade.Buy() or Trade.Buy() is failing with undeclared identifier, check the CTrade setup before changing strategy logic.

I would verify this order:

1. Add #include <Trade/Trade.mqh> near the top of the EA.
2. Declare one object globally: CTrade trade;
3. Use the exact same object name and case everywhere. trade.Buy is not the same as Trade.Buy.
4. Make sure the previous input or variable line ends with a semicolon before CTrade.
5. After Buy/Sell/PositionModify, print trade.ResultRetcode() and trade.ResultRetcodeDescription().

If useful, I wrote a focused CTrade setup checklist here:
https://www.workfusionapp.com/resources/mql5-ctrade-include-trade-object-setup?utm_source=mql5_forum&utm_medium=manual_reply&utm_campaign=ctrade_setup_p0

manualReviewQueue.title: iMA CopyBuffer handle reply
manualReviewQueue.body:
The key point is that MQL5 indicator functions usually return a handle, not the final indicator value like many MT4 examples.

I would structure it this way:

1. Create the indicator handle once in OnInit, for example iMA(...).
2. Check the handle against INVALID_HANDLE.
3. In OnTick, call CopyBuffer(handle, bufferIndex, startPosition, count, array).
4. Check that CopyBuffer returned enough values before reading the array.
5. Use ArraySetAsSeries if you want [0] to represent the current bar and [1] the previous bar.
6. Release the handle in OnDeinit.

If the values look reversed, first confirm the array direction and start position before changing the strategy rule.

If useful, I wrote a focused MQL5 CopyBuffer/indicator-handle checklist here:
https://www.workfusionapp.com/resources/fix-mql5-copybuffer-indicator-handle?utm_source=mql5_forum&utm_medium=manual_reply&utm_campaign=copybuffer_handle_p0

manualReviewQueue.title: Magic-number evaluation reply
manualReviewQueue.body: This can be done from MT5 history if each EA uses a unique Magic Number. Use HistorySelect, loop through deals, group by DEAL_MAGIC, and sum profit/swap/commission while filtering entry types so you do not double-count.
